title: The Tableau Algorithm for Modal Logic

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why truth tables are insufficient for deciding modal satisfiability? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Kripke models are not required to be finite — an infinite number of possible worlds may be needed
Modal logic has no notion of satisfiability, only validity
There is no fixed bound on the number of worlds to check
Simply exhausting all possible models is infeasible
Propositional truth tables with $n$ variables require $2^n$ rows, which is always tractable

explanations.text: Kripke models can have infinitely many worlds, making enumeration impossible.
explanations.notes_quote: Kripke models are not required to be finite — an infinite number of possible worlds may be needed (e.g., in temporal logics where each world represents a future time instant)

explanations.text: Modal logic does have a notion of satisfiability — the tableau algorithm is designed precisely to decide it.
Without a bound on the number of worlds, no exhaustive search is possible.
explanations.notes_quote: There is no fixed bound on the number of worlds to check

explanations.text: Exhausting all possible Kripke models is infeasible because there are infinitely many of them.
explanations.notes_quote: Simply exhausting all possible models is infeasible

explanations.text: While truth tables for propositional logic with $n$ variables have $2^n$ rows, this exponential growth is not 'always tractable' — and more importantly, truth tables cannot handle the unbounded world structure of modal logic.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Tableau Algorithm for modal logic? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It provides a systematic, finite procedure for deciding modal satisfiability and validity
It works by constructing all possible Kripke models up to a fixed size
It is designed to overcome the limitations of truth tables for modal logic
It is only applicable to propositional logic, not modal logic

explanations.text: The tableau algorithm is described as 'a systematic, finite procedure for deciding modal satisfiability and validity'.
explanations.notes_quote: The Tableau Algorithm provides a systematic, finite procedure for deciding modal satisfiability and validity.

explanations.text: The tableau algorithm does not enumerate all Kripke models; instead it systematically decomposes formulas and constructs a witness model only when satisfiable.
The section motivates the tableau algorithm precisely because truth tables are insufficient for modal logic.
explanations.notes_quote: For classical propositional logic, satisfiability and validity are decidable by truth tables... However, for modal logic... The Tableau Algorithm provides a systematic, finite procedure

explanations.text: The tableau algorithm is extended specifically to modal logic via tagged formulas and modal rules — it is not limited to propositional logic.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a propositional tableau? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is a two-column table with a True column and a False column
It is formally a pair $(\mathbf{True}, \mathbf{False}) \in \mathcal{P}(\mathit{Fml}) \times \mathcal{P}(\mathit{Fml})$
A tableau is closed if $\mathbf{True} \cap \mathbf{False} \neq \emptyset$
A tableau is open if it contains a tautology
The True column lists subformulas that must be true; the False column lists subformulas that must be false

explanations.text: A propositional tableau is precisely a two-column table.
explanations.notes_quote: A (propositional) tableau (plural: tableaux) is a two-column table with: A True column: lists all subformulas we want to be true; A False column: lists all subformulas we want to be false

explanations.text: This is the formal definition given in Definition 10.1.
explanations.notes_quote: Formally, a tableau is a pair $(\mathbf{True}, \mathbf{False}) \in \mathcal{P}(\mathit{Fml}) \times \mathcal{P}(\mathit{Fml})$ where True and False are finite subsets of formulas.

explanations.text: Closure is defined as the same formula appearing in both columns — a contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: A tableau is closed if $\mathbf{True} \cap \mathbf{False} \neq \emptyset$ (same formula appears in both columns — contradiction).

explanations.text: A tableau is open if it is NOT closed — i.e., no formula appears in both columns. The presence of a tautology is not the criterion.
This directly describes the role of each column in the tableau.
explanations.notes_quote: A True column: lists all subformulas we want to be true; A False column: lists all subformulas we want to be false

questions.text: To check whether formula $F$ is satisfiable using a propositional tableau, which of the following steps are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Create the initial tableau $( \{F\}, \emptyset )$ with $F$ in the True column
If any branch results in a fully expanded open tableau, $F$ is satisfiable
If every branch results in a closed tableau, $F$ is unsatisfiable
Create the initial tableau $( \emptyset, \{F\} )$ with $F$ in the False column
The satisfying assignment is: $V(p) = 1 \iff p \in \mathbf{True}$

explanations.text: To check satisfiability, F goes in the True column of the initial tableau.
explanations.notes_quote: To decide whether $F \in \mathit{Fml}$ is satisfiable: create the initial tableau $( \{F\}, \emptyset )$ with $F$ in the True column.

explanations.text: An open branch witnesses satisfiability.
explanations.notes_quote: If any branch results in a fully expanded open tableau: $F$ is satisfiable.

explanations.text: If every branch closes, there is no satisfying assignment.
explanations.notes_quote: If every branch of the algorithm results in a closed tableau: $F$ is unsatisfiable.

explanations.text: Placing $F$ in the False column is the setup for checking validity (tautology checking), not satisfiability.
The satisfying assignment is read off directly from the True column.
explanations.notes_quote: Read off the satisfying assignment: $V(p) = 1 \iff p \in \mathbf{True}$.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the tableau rules for the True column? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(\neg_\text{true})$: if $\neg F \in \mathbf{True}$, add $F$ to $\mathbf{False}$
$(\wedge_\text{true})$: if $(F \wedge G) \in \mathbf{True}$, branch into two sub-tableaux: one with $F$ in True, one with $G$ in True
$(\vee_\text{true})$: if $(F \vee G) \in \mathbf{True}$, branch into two sub-tableaux: one with $F$ in True, one with $G$ in True
$(\to_\text{true})$: if $(F \to G) \in \mathbf{True}$, branch: one with $F$ in False OR one with $G$ in True
$(\vee_\text{true})$: if $(F \vee G) \in \mathbf{True}$, add both $F$ and $G$ to True (non-branching)

explanations.text: The negation-true rule moves the negated formula to the False column.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
explanations.notes_quote:
$(
eg_	ext{true})$: If $
eg F$ appears in True, move $F$ to False and mark $
eg F$ as expanded

explanations.text: $(\wedge_\text{true})$ is a NON-branching rule: both conjuncts are added to the SAME branch in True. Branching is the behaviour of $(\vee_\text{true})$ and $(\wedge_\text{false})$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column

explanations.text: Disjunction in True is a branching rule — each disjunct goes to a separate branch.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\vee_\text{true})$: If $(F \vee G)$ appears in True, branch into two sub-tableaux: one with $F$ added to True, one with $G$ added to True

explanations.text: Implication in True branches: either the antecedent is false (making the implication trivially true) or the consequent is true.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\to_\text{true})$: If $(F \to G)$ appears in True, branch: one branch with $F$ in False (i.e., $F$ is false, making $F\to G$ trivially true), one branch with $G$ in True

explanations.text: $(\vee_\text{true})$ is a branching rule, not a non-branching one. Adding both to the same branch would be the rule for $(\wedge_\text{true})$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column

questions.source_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
questions.text: For the $(\wedge_\text{true})$ rule: if $(F \wedge G) \in \mathbf{True}$, what happens? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Both $F$ and $G$ are added to True on the same branch (non-branching)
$(F \wedge G)$ is marked as expanded (crossed out)
The tableau branches into two: one with $F$ in True, one with $G$ in True
Both $F$ and $G$ are added to False

explanations.text: Conjunction in True is a non-branching rule — both conjuncts are added to the same branch.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\wedge_\text{true})$: If $(F \wedge G)$ appears in True, add both conjuncts $F$ and $G$ to True and mark $(F\wedge G)$ as expanded

explanations.text: Once expanded, the formula is marked (crossed out) so it is not re-applied.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column
explanations.notes_quote: add both conjuncts $F$ and $G$ to True and mark $(F\wedge G)$ as expanded

explanations.text: Branching occurs for $(\vee_\text{true})$ and $(\to_\text{true})$, not $(\wedge_\text{true})$. Conjunction is true iff both conjuncts are true — no branching needed.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column

explanations.text: Adding both to False would be incorrect; the rule adds them to True since the conjunction is assumed true.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.3: Rules for the True Column

questions.source_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state rules for the False column? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(\neg_\text{false})$: if $\neg F \in \mathbf{False}$, add $F$ to $\mathbf{True}$
$(\wedge_\text{false})$: if $(F \wedge G) \in \mathbf{False}$, branch into $F \in \mathbf{False}$ or $G \in \mathbf{False}$
$(\vee_\text{false})$: if $(F \vee G) \in \mathbf{False}$, branch into $F \in \mathbf{False}$ or $G \in \mathbf{False}$
$(\to_\text{false})$: if $(F \to G) \in \mathbf{False}$, add $F$ to $\mathbf{True}$ and $G$ to $\mathbf{False}$ (non-branching)
$(\vee_\text{false})$: if $(F \vee G) \in \mathbf{False}$, add both $F$ and $G$ to $\mathbf{False}$ (non-branching)

explanations.text: If a negation is false, the inner formula must be true.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\neg_\text{false})$: If $\neg F$ appears in False, move $F$ to True

explanations.text: A conjunction is false iff at least one conjunct is false — hence branching.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\wedge_\text{false})$: $(F \wedge G)$ is false iff $F$ is false or $G$ is false. Branch

explanations.text: $(\vee_\text{false})$ is NON-branching: a disjunction is false iff BOTH disjuncts are false, so both go to False on the same branch.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column

explanations.text: An implication is false iff the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\to_\text{false})$: $(F \to G)$ is false iff $F$ is true and $G$ is false. Add $F$ to True and $G$ to False

explanations.text: A disjunction is false iff both disjuncts are false — both are added to False on the same branch (non-branching).
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.2.4: Rules for the False Column
explanations.notes_quote: $(\vee_\text{false})$: $(F \vee G)$ is false iff both $F$ and $G$ are false. Add both to False

questions.text: To check whether $F$ is a tautology (valid) using the tableau algorithm, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Place $F$ in the False column of the initial tableau $( \emptyset, \{F\} )$
$F$ is valid iff every fully expanded branch is closed (no open branch)
$F$ is valid iff $\neg F$ is not satisfiable
Place $F$ in the True column to check validity
A single open branch when $F$ is in False provides a counter-model to $F$

explanations.text: Validity checking places F in the False column to search for a counter-model.
explanations.notes_quote: Place $F$ in the False column of the initial tableau $(\emptyset, \{F\})$ and expand.

explanations.text: If every branch closes, there is no counter-model, confirming validity.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid iff every fully expanded branch is closed (no open branch corresponds to a counter-model).

explanations.text: This equivalence is the fundamental connection between validity and satisfiability.
explanations.notes_quote: $F \text{ is valid} \iff \neg F \text{ is NOT satisfiable}$

explanations.text: Placing $F$ in the True column is for checking satisfiability, not validity. Validity requires placing $F$ in the False column.
An open branch when $F$ is in False witnesses a satisfying assignment for $\neg F$, which is a counter-model to $F$.
explanations.notes_quote: no open branch corresponds to a counter-model

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe tagged formulas in modal tableaux? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A tagged formula is a pair $(w : F)$ where $w$ is a world label and $F$ is a modal formula
World labels track which world a formula must be true or false at
Tagged formulas are only used in the True column, not the False column
The label $w$ designates a specific world in the to-be-determined Kripke model
Tagged formulas eliminate the need for a Relation column in modal tableaux

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of a tagged formula.
explanations.notes_quote: A tagged formula is a pair $(w : F)$ where: $w$ is a label designating a specific world in the to-be-determined Kripke model; $F \in \mathit{Fml}^\Box$ is a modal formula

explanations.text: In modal logic, satisfiability varies world-by-world, so tagged formulas track which world is involved.
explanations.notes_quote: In modal logic, satisfiability varies world-by-world. We must track not just whether a subformula should be true/false, but at which world.

explanations.text: Tagged formulas appear in both the True and False columns — a modal tableau is closed when a tagged formula appears in both columns.
The label $w$ represents a specific world in the Kripke model being constructed.
explanations.notes_quote: $w$ is a label designating a specific world in the to-be-determined Kripke model

explanations.text: Tagged formulas do NOT eliminate the Relation column — a modal tableau has three columns: True, False, and Relation.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a modal tableau? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A modal tableau has three columns: True, False, and Relation
The Relation column stores pairs $(w, u)$ that must be in the accessibility relation $R$
A modal tableau is closed if a tagged formula $(w:F)$ appears in both True and False
A modal tableau has only two columns: True and False
The Relation column stores truth values of propositional variables

explanations.text: Modal tableaux extend propositional tableaux with a third Relation column.
explanations.notes_quote: A (modal) tableau is a three-column table with: A True column: tagged (sub)formulas that must be true; A False column: tagged (sub)formulas that must be false; A Relation column: relations $(w, u)$ that must be in the accessibility relation $R$

explanations.text: The Relation column accumulates pairs from the accessibility relation as the algorithm runs.
explanations.notes_quote: A Relation column: relations $(w, u)$ that must be in the accessibility relation $R$

explanations.text: Closure in modal tableaux requires the same tagged formula to appear in both columns — a contradiction at the same world.
explanations.notes_quote: A modal tableau is closed if $\mathbf{True} \cap \mathbf{False} \neq \emptyset$ (a tagged formula $(w:F)$ appears in both columns)

explanations.text: Modal tableaux have three columns — the Relation column is essential for tracking the accessibility relation.
Truth values of propositional variables are tracked via tagged formulas in the True and False columns, not the Relation column.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over
questions.text: How do the propositional tableau rules extend to modal tableaux? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: All eight propositional rules remain valid in modal tableaux
The rules are applied to tagged formulas instead of plain formulas
For example, $(\to_\text{true})$ on $(w:(F\to G)) \in \mathbf{True}$ branches into $(w:F) \in \mathbf{False}$ or $(w:G) \in \mathbf{True}$
The propositional rules are completely replaced by four new modal rules
World labels are ignored when applying propositional rules in modal tableaux

explanations.text: All eight propositional rules carry over to modal tableaux unchanged in structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over
explanations.notes_quote: All eight propositional rules remain valid, but now applied to tagged formulas.

explanations.text: The same rules apply but now to tagged formula pairs $(w:F)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over
explanations.notes_quote: All eight propositional rules remain valid, but now applied to tagged formulas.

explanations.text: This is the direct example given in the notes for how a propositional rule carries over.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over
explanations.notes_quote: $(\to_\text{true})$ becomes: if $(w:(F\to G)) \in \mathbf{True}$, branch into $(w:F) \in \mathbf{False}$ or $(w:G) \in \mathbf{True}$

explanations.text: The propositional rules are NOT replaced — they all carry over. The four modal rules are additional rules for $\Box$ and $\Diamond$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over

explanations.text: World labels are preserved throughout — a rule applied to $(w:F)$ produces tagged formulas at the same world $w$ (for propositional rules) or at a new world (for modal rules).
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.3: All Propositional Rules Carry Over

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ modal tableau rule? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $(w:\Diamond F) \in \mathbf{True}$, introduce a fresh label $u$ and add $(u:F)$ to True and $(w,u)$ to Relation
The new label $u$ must be fresh — not appearing elsewhere on the current branch
If $(w:\Diamond F) \in \mathbf{True}$, add $(w:F)$ to True (no new world needed)
$(\Diamond_\text{true})$ uses sequential numbering $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ for fresh labels
$(\Diamond_\text{true})$ should NOT be marked as expanded after application

explanations.text: This is the precise statement of the $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ rule.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Diamond_\text{true})$: If $(w:\Diamond F)$ appears in True, there must exist some accessible world $u$ where $F$ is true. Introduce a fresh label $u$: add $(u:F)$ to True and $(w,u)$ to Relation

explanations.text: Freshness is critical — reusing an existing label would conflate distinct worlds and corrupt the model.
explanations.notes_quote: IMPORTANT: $u$ must be a fresh label — use sequential numbering $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ and take the next available index each time $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ is applied.

explanations.text: Adding $(w:F)$ to True would be wrong — $\Diamond F$ is true at $w$ means $F$ is true at some ACCESSIBLE world, not at $w$ itself.
Sequential numbering ensures freshness across the branch.
explanations.notes_quote: use sequential numbering $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ and take the next available index each time $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ is applied

explanations.text: $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ CAN be marked as expanded once applied. It is $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ that must NOT be marked as expanded, as they may need re-application.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the $(\Box_\text{false})$ rule? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $(w:\Box F) \in \mathbf{False}$, introduce a fresh label $u$, add $(u:F)$ to False and $(w,u)$ to Relation
$(\Box_\text{false})$ introduces a fresh world because there must exist some accessible world where $F$ is false
If $(w:\Box F) \in \mathbf{False}$, add $(w:F)$ to False at the same world
$(\Box_\text{false})$ should NOT be marked as expanded, unlike $(\Box_\text{true})$

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of the $(\Box_\text{false})$ rule.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Box_\text{false})$: If $(w:\Box F)$ appears in False, there must exist some accessible world where $F$ is false. Introduce a fresh label $u$: add $(u:F)$ to False and $(w,u)$ to Relation

explanations.text: $\Box F$ being false means the universal claim fails at some accessible world.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Box_\text{false})$: If $(w:\Box F)$ appears in False, there must exist some accessible world where $F$ is false.

explanations.text: Adding at the same world would be wrong — $\Box F$ being false requires a witness world accessible from $w$, not $w$ itself.
$(\Box_\text{false})$ introduces a new world and CAN be marked as expanded. It is $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ that must NOT be marked as expanded.

questions.text: Why must $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ NOT be marked as expanded after application? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Because additional relations $(w, u)$ may be added to Relation later, requiring re-application
Because marking them expanded would cause the algorithm to loop infinitely
Because $\Box F$ must be true at all accessible worlds, and new accessible worlds may be introduced
Because these rules do not introduce fresh world labels and must be kept active
Because $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagate across existing relations — and new relations may be added

explanations.text: New world-pairs may be added by later applications of $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ or $(\Box_\text{false})$, requiring $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ to be re-applied.
explanations.notes_quote: CRITICAL: Do NOT mark $(w:\Box F)$ as expanded. It may need to be re-applied if additional relations $(w,u)$ are added to Relation later.

explanations.text: Not marking them as expanded does not cause infinite looping — termination is still guaranteed by other means (though transitive frames require loop-checking).
$(\Box_\text{true})$ must be applied to every accessible world — since new worlds can be introduced, it cannot be finalized until no more relations are added.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Box_\text{true})$: If $(w:\Box F)$ in True and $(w,u_1),\ldots,(w,u_n) \in$ Relation: add $(u_i:F)$ to True for each. CRITICAL: Do NOT mark as expanded.

explanations.text: $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ do NOT introduce fresh worlds; the reason they cannot be marked expanded is that new relations may be added later that require them to fire again.
They propagate across current relations, and future rules may add new relations, requiring re-propagation.
explanations.notes_quote: CRITICAL: Do NOT mark $(w:\Diamond F)$ as expanded. It may need to be re-applied if additional relations are added.

questions.text: What does the $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ rule do? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $(w:\Diamond F) \in \mathbf{False}$, for each $(w, u_i)$ in Relation, add $(u_i:F)$ to False
It introduces a fresh world label $u$ and adds $(u:F)$ to False
It must NOT be marked as expanded after application
$\Diamond F$ being false at $w$ means $F$ must be false at every accessible world from $w$

explanations.text: $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagates falsity to all accessible worlds already in Relation.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Diamond_\text{false})$: If $(w:\Diamond F)$ appears in False: for each $(w,u_i)$ in Relation, add $(u_i:F)$ to False

explanations.text: Introducing a fresh world is what $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ and $(\Box_\text{false})$ do. $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagates across EXISTING relations.
Like $(\Box_\text{true})$, it may need re-application if new relations are added.
explanations.notes_quote: CRITICAL: Do NOT mark $(w:\Diamond F)$ as expanded. It may need to be re-applied if additional relations are added.

explanations.text: $\Diamond F$ is false iff F is false at every accessible world — this is the semantic justification of the rule.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Diamond_\text{false})$: If $(w:\Diamond F)$ appears in False, then $F$ must be false at every accessible world.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
questions.text: How is a satisfying Kripke model read off from a fully expanded open modal tableau branch? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $W = \{w_i : (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{True} \cup \mathbf{False}\}$ (the set of all world labels appearing)
$(w_i, w_j) \in R \iff (w_i, w_j) \in \mathbf{Relation}$
$V(w_i, p) = 1 \iff (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{True}$
$V(w_i, p) = 1 \iff (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{False}$
Models produced by the tableau algorithm always have a tree structure

explanations.text: The set of worlds is exactly the set of world labels appearing in the tableau.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
explanations.notes_quote: $W = \{w_i : (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{True} \cup \mathbf{False}\}$

explanations.text: The accessibility relation is read directly from the Relation column.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
explanations.notes_quote: $(w_i, w_j) \in R \iff (w_i, w_j) \in \mathbf{Relation}$

explanations.text: A propositional variable is true at a world iff the tagged formula appears in the True column.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
explanations.notes_quote: $V(w_i, p) = 1 \iff (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{True}$

explanations.text: Variables in the False column are false at that world. The correct reading is: $V(w_i, p) = 1 \iff (w_i : p) \in \mathbf{True}$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model

explanations.text: This is the Tree Model Property stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
explanations.notes_quote: Tree Model Property: Models produced by the tableau algorithm always have a tree structure. Every satisfiable modal formula has a tree model.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the initial tableau setup for modal tableaux? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: To check satisfiability of $F$: initial tableau with $(w_0 : F)$ in True, everything else empty
To check validity of $F$: initial tableau with $(w_0 : F)$ in False
$F$ is valid iff every branch in the False-column expansion closes
To check satisfiability of $F$: initial tableau with $(w_0 : F)$ in False
$F$ is valid w.r.t. $\mathcal{C}$ iff $\neg F$ is NOT satisfiable w.r.t. $\mathcal{C}$

explanations.text: Satisfiability checking starts with the formula in the True column at the initial world $w_0$.
explanations.notes_quote: To check satisfiability of $F$: Initial tableau with $(w_0 : F)$ in True, everything else empty.

explanations.text: Validity checking places $F$ in the False column to search for a counter-model.
explanations.notes_quote: To check validity of $F$: Use $F$ valid $\iff$ $\neg F$ not satisfiable. Initial tableau with $(w_0 : F)$ in False and apply rules

explanations.text: If every branch closes when $F$ is in False, no counter-model exists, so $F$ is valid.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is valid iff every branch is closed.

explanations.text: Placing $F$ in the False column is for checking validity, not satisfiability.
This is the fundamental equivalence that underpins validity checking for frame classes.
explanations.notes_quote: $F \text{ is valid with respect to } \mathcal{C} \iff \neg F \text{ is NOT satisfiable with respect to } \mathcal{C}$

sections.title: 10.4 Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames

questions.source_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
questions.text: To check satisfiability with respect to a specific class of frames, which of the following modifications to the tableau algorithm are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: After each application of $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ or $(\Box_\text{false})$, close the Relation set under the additional frame constraints
For reflexive frames: after adding world label $w_i$, also add $(w_i, w_i)$ to Relation
For transitive frames: whenever $(x,y)$ and $(y,z)$ are in Relation, also add $(x,z)$
Frame-restricted tableau checks do not require any modification to the base algorithm
The extracted model from an open branch will satisfy the required frame property due to the Relation closure

explanations.text: After relation-adding rules, the Relation set must be closed under the required frame constraints.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: after each application of $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ or $(\Box_\text{false})$ — which add new relations — close the Relation set under the additional constraints required by the frame class.

explanations.text: Reflexivity requires every world to be related to itself.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: Example — Reflexive frames: After adding world label $w_i$, also add $(w_i, w_i)$ to Relation.

explanations.text: Transitivity requires closing under composition of relations.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: Example — Transitive frames: Whenever $(x,y)$ and $(y,z)$ are added to Relation, also add $(x,z)$ to ensure transitivity.

explanations.text: Frame-restricted satisfiability DOES require modification — specifically the closure of the Relation set under the relevant frame properties.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames

explanations.text: By closing Relation under the required properties during the algorithm, any model extracted from an open branch will satisfy those properties.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: This guarantees that any model read from an open tableau will be transitive.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
questions.text: Regarding the looping issue on transitive frames, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The formula $F = \Box(p \to \Diamond q) \wedge \Box(q \to \Diamond p) \wedge \Diamond p$ can cause the transitive tableau to loop indefinitely
Loop-checking (blocking) resolves this by reusing existing world labels when the formula set at a new world matches an existing one
The looping produces an infinite chain $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ alternating $p$ and $q$ values
The looping issue means transitive modal logic is undecidable
Blocking/loop-checking ensures termination while preserving completeness

explanations.text: This specific formula is given as the example that causes looping on transitive frames.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: Issue with transitive frames and looping: Some satisfiable formulas on transitive frames can cause the tableau algorithm to loop... For example, $F = \Box(p \to \Diamond q) \wedge \Box(q \to \Diamond p) \wedge \Diamond p$

explanations.text: Blocking/loop-checking resolves termination by reusing world labels when formula sets repeat.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: A fix is to reuse existing world labels when the formula being added to a world already appears there (a blocking or loop-checking mechanism).

explanations.text: The looping creates an infinite alternating chain of worlds.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: causing an infinite chain $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ alternating $p$ and $q$

explanations.text: Transitive modal logic (K4) is decidable — the looping issue is a problem with a naive implementation of the tableau algorithm, not with decidability of the logic itself.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames

explanations.text: Blocking terminates the algorithm while still correctly identifying satisfiable formulas.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.4: Deciding Satisfiability w.r.t. a Class of Frames
explanations.notes_quote: This blocking condition ensures termination while preserving completeness.

questions.text: For $F_1 = \Diamond(p \vee q) \wedge \Box\neg p \wedge \Box(q \to \Diamond r)$, which of the following are true in the tableau analysis? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The initial tableau has $(w_0 : F_1)$ in True
Applying $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ introduces a fresh world $w_1$ with $(w_1:(p\vee q))$ in True
Branch A (where $(w_1:p)$ in True) closes because $(w_1:\neg p)$ also holds in True
$F_1$ is unsatisfiable
A witness model for $F_1$ includes worlds $\{w_0, w_1, w_2\}$ with $V(w_1,q)=1$ and $V(w_2,r)=1$

explanations.text: The standard initial setup places the formula to test in True at $w_0$.
explanations.notes_quote: Initial tableau: $(w_0 : F_1)$ in True.

explanations.text: The $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ rule creates a fresh world $w_1$ for the diamond formula.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ to $(w_0:\Diamond(p\vee q))$: introduce fresh $w_1$. Add $(w_1:(p\vee q))$ to True and $(w_0,w_1)$ to Relation.

explanations.text: Branch A closes because $p$ must be both true (from the branch) and false (from $\Box\neg p$) at $w_1$.
explanations.notes_quote: Branch A: $(w_1:p)$ in True. But $(w_1:\neg p)$ also in True, so $(w_1:p)$ would go to False — closed (contradiction).

explanations.text: $F_1$ is satisfiable — Branch B leads to an open tableau with a witness model.
explanations.notes_quote: $F_1$ is satisfiable.

explanations.text: This is the witness model explicitly given in the worked example.

questions.text: For $F_2 = \Box(p \to \Diamond q) \wedge \Diamond(p \wedge \Box\neg q)$, which of the following are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F_2$ is unsatisfiable
The tableau introduces world $w_1$ with $(w_1:p)$ and $(w_1:\Box\neg q)$ in True
The contradiction arises because $(w_2:q)$ is in both True and False
$F_2$ is satisfiable with two worlds
Both branches of the expansion close

explanations.text: $F_2$ is explicitly shown to be unsatisfiable in the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: $F_2$ is unsatisfiable.

explanations.text: Expanding $(w_1:(p\wedge\Box\neg q))$ yields both $(w_1:p)$ and $(w_1:\Box\neg q)$ in True.
explanations.notes_quote: Expand $(w_1:(p\wedge\Box\neg q))$: add $(w_1:p)$ and $(w_1:\Box\neg q)$ to True.

explanations.text: $w_2:q$ is added to True (from $\Diamond q$) and then to False (from $\Box\neg q$ at $w_1$), closing that branch.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\Box_\text{true})$ to $(w_1:\Box\neg q)$ with $(w_1,w_2)$: add $(w_2:\neg q)$ to True, which means $(w_2:q)$ in False. But $(w_2:q)$ in True — closed.

explanations.text: $F_2$ is unsatisfiable — no model of any size satisfies it.
Both Branch A (p false vs p true) and Branch B (the q/¬q contradiction) close.
explanations.notes_quote: Branch A: $(w_1:p)$ in False. But $(w_1:p)$ in True — closed. ... But $(w_2:q)$ in True — closed.

questions.text: To prove $A_K = \Box(p \to q) \to (\Box p \to \Box q)$ is valid over all frames, which of the following steps in the tableau are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Place $A_K$ in the False column of the initial tableau
Applying $(\to_\text{false})$ twice yields $(w_0:\Box(p\to q))$ in True, $(w_0:\Box p)$ in True, and $(w_0:\Box q)$ in False
Applying $(\Box_\text{false})$ to $(w_0:\Box q)$ introduces fresh $w_1$ with $(w_1:q)$ in False
Branch A (where $(w_1:p)$ is in False) is open
Both branches close, proving $A_K$ is valid in all frames

explanations.text: To check validity, place the formula in False to search for a counter-model.
explanations.notes_quote: To show $A_K$ is valid over all frames, place $A_K$ in False in the initial tableau

explanations.text: Two applications of $(\to_\text{false})$ decompose the nested implication.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\to_\text{false})$: $(w_0:\Box(p\to q))$ in True, $(w_0:(\Box p \to \Box q))$ in False. Apply $(\to_\text{false})$: $(w_0:\Box p)$ in True, $(w_0:\Box q)$ in False.

explanations.text: $(\Box_\text{false})$ introduces a fresh world where $q$ is false.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\Box_\text{false})$ to $(w_0:\Box q)$: introduce $w_1$, add $(w_1:q)$ to False and $(w_0,w_1)$ to Relation.

explanations.text: Branch A (where $(w_1:p)$ is in False) is CLOSED because $(w_1:p)$ is also in True (from $(\Box_\text{true})$ on $(w_0:\Box p)$).
explanations.notes_quote: Branch A: $(w_1:p)$ in False. But $(w_1:p)$ in True — closed.

explanations.text: All branches close, confirming $A_K$ is valid in all frames.
explanations.notes_quote: Both branches closed. Therefore $\neg A_K$ is unsatisfiable, so $A_K$ is valid in all frames.

questions.source_ref: Section 10.5: SGT 10.3 — Validity over Transitive Frames
questions.text: To prove $A_4 = \Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ valid over transitive frames, which of the following are true of the tableau proof? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Place $A_4$ in False: $(w_0:\Diamond\Diamond p)$ in True and $(w_0:\Diamond p)$ in False
$(\Diamond_\text{true})$ introduces $w_1$ with $(w_1:\Diamond p)$ in True; transitivity later adds $(w_0, w_2)$ to Relation
$(\Diamond_\text{false})$ applied to $(w_0:\Diamond p)$ with relation $(w_0,w_2)$ yields $(w_2:p)$ in False, causing a contradiction
The proof works over all frames, not just transitive ones
Transitivity closure is the key step that closes the tableau

explanations.text: Validity checking places the formula in False, and $(\to_\text{false})$ decomposes the implication.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\to_\text{false})$: $(w_0:\Diamond\Diamond p)$ in True, $(w_0:\Diamond p)$ in False.

explanations.text: $w_1$ is introduced by $(\Diamond_\text{true})$, and then $w_2$ is introduced from $w_1$, after which transitivity adds $(w_0,w_2)$.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ to $(w_0:\Diamond\Diamond p)$: introduce $w_1$... Transitive closure: $(w_0,w_1),(w_1,w_2) \in R \Rightarrow$ add $(w_0,w_2)$ to Relation.

explanations.text: Once $(w_0,w_2)$ is in Relation, $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ forces $(w_2:p)$ into False, contradicting $(w_2:p)$ in True.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ to $(w_0:\Diamond p)$ with $(w_0,w_2)$: add $(w_2:p)$ to False. But $(w_2:p)$ in True — closed.

explanations.text: $A_4$ (in this $\Diamond\Diamond p \to \Diamond p$ form) is only valid over transitive frames — the proof relies on the transitivity closure step.
explanations.notes_quote: $A_4$ is valid over all transitive frames.

explanations.text: Without transitivity, the relation $(w_0,w_2)$ would not be added, and the branch might remain open.
explanations.notes_quote: Transitive closure: $(w_0,w_1),(w_1,w_2) \in R \Rightarrow$ add $(w_0,w_2)$ to Relation.

questions.text: Regarding the looping issue in SGT 10.4 with $F = \Box(p \to \Diamond q) \wedge \Box(q \to \Diamond p) \wedge \Diamond p$ on transitive frames, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The formula $F$ causes an infinite chain $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ alternating $p$ and $q$
The blocking fix checks whether a new world's formula set already matches an existing world on the branch
If blocking applies, the algorithm reuses an existing world label instead of creating a new one
The looping proves $F$ is unsatisfiable on transitive frames
Blocking (loop-checking) ensures termination while preserving completeness

explanations.text: The looping generates new worlds at each step, alternating between $p$ and $q$.
explanations.notes_quote: This repeats indefinitely: $w_4, w_5, \ldots$ ... causing an infinite chain $w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots$ alternating $p$ and $q$

explanations.text: The blocking condition is: check if the new world's formula set matches an existing world on the branch.
explanations.notes_quote: check whether there already exists a world $w_j$ on the current branch that has the exact same set of formulas labelled to it

explanations.text: Reusing the existing world label prevents infinite generation of new worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: reuse $w_j$ (set $(w_i,w_j)$ in Relation) instead of creating a new world

explanations.text: $F$ is satisfiable on transitive frames — the looping is a problem with the algorithm's naive implementation, not with the formula itself.
Blocking preserves the algorithm's soundness and completeness while guaranteeing termination.
explanations.notes_quote: This blocking condition ensures termination while preserving completeness.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match tableau terms to their definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A **branching rule** splits the tableau into two sub-tableaux (e.g., $(\vee_\text{true})$, $(\wedge_\text{false})$)
A **non-branching rule** adds formulas to the current tableau without splitting (e.g., $(\wedge_\text{true})$, $(\vee_\text{false})$)
A **fresh label** is a world label already appearing elsewhere in the tableau branch
The **Tree Model Property** states that every satisfiable modal formula is satisfiable in a tree-shaped model
**Transitive closure** adds $(x,z)$ to Relation whenever $(x,y)$ and $(y,z)$ are both in Relation

explanations.text: Branching rules split the tree — disjunction in True and conjunction in False both require branching.
explanations.notes_quote: Branching rule: A tableau rule that splits into two sub-tableaux (e.g., $\vee_\text{true}$, $\wedge_\text{false}$)

explanations.text: Non-branching rules add formulas to the same branch — conjunction in True and disjunction in False are examples.
explanations.notes_quote: Non-branching rule: A tableau rule that adds formulas to the current tableau without splitting (e.g., $\wedge_\text{true}$, $\vee_\text{false}$)

explanations.text: A fresh label is one that does NOT appear elsewhere on the current branch — it is new and unique.
The Tree Model Property is explicitly stated in the notes and is a consequence of the tableau construction.
explanations.notes_quote: Tree model property: Every satisfiable modal formula is satisfiable in a model whose frame is a tree

explanations.text: This is the definition of transitive closure applied to the Relation column.
explanations.notes_quote: Transitive closure: When checking satisfiability w.r.t. transitive frames: if $(x,y),(y,z)$ in Relation, add $(x,z)$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the four modal tableau rules and their key properties? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ and $(\Box_\text{false})$ introduce fresh world labels
$(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagate across existing relations and must NOT be marked as expanded
$(\Diamond_\text{true})$ propagates across existing relations; $(\Box_\text{false})$ introduces fresh labels
All four modal rules introduce fresh world labels
$(\Box_\text{true})$ adds $(u_i:F)$ to True for each $(w,u_i)$ already in Relation

explanations.text: Both of these rules witness the existence of an accessible world, so they introduce fresh labels.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ rule: If $(w:\Diamond F)$ in True: add fresh $u$, put $(u:F)$ in True, add $(w,u)$ to Relation. $(\Box_\text{false})$ rule: If $(w:\Box F)$ in False: add fresh $u$, put $(u:F)$ in False, add $(w,u)$ to Relation

explanations.text: These rules universally quantify over accessible worlds, and must fire again if new relations are added.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Box_\text{true})$ rule: If $(w:\Box F)$ in True: for each $(w,u_i)$ in Relation, add $(u_i:F)$ to True. Do NOT mark as expanded. $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ rule: If $(w:\Diamond F)$ in False: for each $(w,u_i)$ in Relation, add $(u_i:F)$ to False. Do NOT mark as expanded.

explanations.text: This is reversed: $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ introduces fresh labels; $(\Box_\text{false})$ also introduces fresh labels. $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagate across existing relations.
Only $(\Diamond_\text{true})$ and $(\Box_\text{false})$ introduce fresh labels. $(\Box_\text{true})$ and $(\Diamond_\text{false})$ propagate across existing relations.
$(\Box_\text{true})$ applies to all currently known accessible worlds from $w$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\Box_\text{true})$ rule: If $(w:\Box F)$ in True: for each $(w,u_i)$ in Relation, add $(u_i:F)$ to True.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish propositional tableaux from modal tableaux? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Propositional tableaux have two columns (True, False); modal tableaux have three columns (True, False, Relation)
Modal tableaux use tagged formulas $(w:F)$ whereas propositional tableaux use plain formulas
Modal tableaux have four additional rules for $\Box$ and $\Diamond$ beyond the eight propositional rules
Propositional tableaux can check modal validity but not modal satisfiability
The closure condition differs: propositional tableaux close when a formula appears in both columns; modal tableaux close when a tagged formula appears in both columns

explanations.text: The Relation column is the key structural addition in modal tableaux.
explanations.notes_quote: Tableau: A two-column (propositional) or three-column (modal) table with True, False, and (for modal) Relation columns

explanations.text: Tagged formulas track which world a formula holds at — essential in modal logic.
explanations.notes_quote: Tagged formula: A pair $(w:F)$ linking a modal formula $F$ to a world label $w$

explanations.text: The four modal rules $(\Diamond_\text{true}), (\Box_\text{false}), (\Box_\text{true}), (\Diamond_\text{false})$ extend the eight propositional rules.
explanations.notes_quote: There are four additional rules for formulas involving $\Box$ and $\Diamond$

explanations.text: Propositional tableaux cannot check modal validity or satisfiability — they lack the world-tracking apparatus needed for modal logic.
The closure condition is analogous but uses tagged formulas in the modal case to ensure the contradiction is at the same world.
explanations.notes_quote: A modal tableau is closed if $\mathbf{True} \cap \mathbf{False} \neq \emptyset$ (a tagged formula $(w:F)$ appears in both columns)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly summarise the learning objectives for Week 10? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A formula $F$ is valid iff placing $F$ in the False column leads to every branch closing
The algorithm terminates when all formulas are expanded AND every $\Box$-True and $\Diamond$-False formula has been applied to all relations
Frame-restricted satisfiability requires closing the Relation set under the frame properties after each relation-adding rule
The propositional tableau algorithm has exactly four rules in total
On transitive frames, blocking/loop-checking (reusing world labels when formula sets repeat) ensures termination

explanations.text: This is the validity-checking procedure stated in the summary.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 2 (Tautology checking): $F$ is valid iff $\neg F$ is unsatisfiable. Place $F$ in False and run the algorithm; validity holds iff every branch closes.

explanations.text: This is the termination condition for modal tableaux.
explanations.section_ref: Section 10.3.5: Termination and Reading off a Model
explanations.notes_quote: The algorithm terminates when: All formulas in True and False have been fully expanded, AND every $\Box$ formula in True and every $\Diamond$ formula in False has been applied to all relations in Relation

explanations.text: Frame-restricted checks require Relation closure after each relation-adding step.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 6 (Frame-restricted satisfiability): After each relation-adding rule, close Relation under the required frame property

explanations.text: The propositional tableau algorithm has EIGHT rules (four for the True column and four for the False column), not four.
Blocking resolves the infinite-looping problem on transitive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 8 (Termination issues): On transitive frames, some satisfiable formulas can cause infinite branching. Blocking/loop-checking (reusing world labels when formula sets repeat) resolves this.
